title: Cirrus API integration out of control?
body:
Hi there,

It seems you are using an API integration with Cirrus CI to re-run tasks automatically and it seems it's out of control. See [this task](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6337414148390912), for example.

As far as we can see on out side there is an API requests coming that constantly re-run tasks. We temporary disabled token ending with `2acs` as it seems to us that this behaviour is not intentional and just drains your GCP budget.

comments.body:
@fkorotkov Thanks for raising this and disabling the token. I created https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/6036 to disable the task which triggers the re-runs AFAICT.

We need more time to figure out why this script is misbehaving but you can keep the token disabled for now.

FYI we have [builtin hooks](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/programming-tasks/#hooks)  that you can use for re-run of tasks automatically. These hooks also have a prevention mechanism from infinite loops.

Yes I saw that a while back when i was looking at simplifying cirrus.yml to have less duplication.
Starlark looks nice but it is a lot of work to migrate and test and given we currently have no dedicated Person for CI this is not something we find time for.

@Luap99 FWIW, this exact same task runs on a bunch of other repos. Basically every one that has release-branch CI setup.  I did not look at the other repos to see if a similar haywire situation happened.  Also to close the loop, there's [a CI test](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/.github/actions/check_cirrus_cron/test.sh) for the job's script, run in [Podman CI's "prebuild" script](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/cirrus/prebuild.sh#L78).  Clearly there's some missing test coverage.  However, TBH I would consider just disabling/removing the re-run job everywhere.  That may result in more short-term manual re-runs, but if the desire is to get release branches using TMT anyway, the trade-off may be profitable.

To use the hooks there is no need to migrate from YAML. Starlark and YAML configs can work seamlessly but "hooks" is a separate thing. In the example above it automatically re-runs tasks if they failed and logs contain "Time out" substring. Kind of an easy lambda for common flakes.
@cevich Thanks, yes I am aware the same script is used on the other repos and I have a note to take care of it when I get around to it. I simply have to much stuff to work on right now.

> I did not look at the other repos to see if a similar haywire situation happened.

Skopeo https://github.com/containers/skopeo/actions/runs/13621572958/job/38071884750#step:4:900 “Rerunning 166 tasks”

Podman https://github.com/containers/podman/actions/runs/13425964643/job/37508857900#step:4:357 : 55 tasks, I didn’t find obviously unexpected numbers otherwise.
Thanks for checking Miloslav, yeah I'd say probably good to disable it everywhere.  Something's clearly very broken.

> To use the hooks there is no need to migrate from YAML. Starlark and YAML configs can work seamlessly but "hooks" is a separate thing. 

@fkorotkov sorry I should have explained better.  The context of this issue mainly applies to RHEL release-tracking branches.  The broken re-run script was only ever really a "band-aid" to address old and flaky tests.  So simply turning it off is likely an easier solution, as the tracking branches are likely to move onto a different CI system that better aligns with RHEL (as opposed to Fedora).  But thanks for the help and suggestions nonetheless.
